does: This paper presents a method to improve safety in Large Reasoning Models by triggering their latent ability to recognize and respond to safety concerns. The approach uses two-stage fine-tuning: first, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) teaches models to insert explicit safety tags that trigger deeper analysis of potentially harmful queries, while keeping standard responses unchanged for benign inputs. Second, Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) refines the quality and consistency of the safety analysis generated by the model. Critically, all training data comes from model-generated responses rather than external manual annotation, making the approach scalable and reducing dependence on human-labeled safety data.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in understanding emergent reasoning capabilities in large language models and extends prior safety alignment research that relies on supervised fine-tuning and preference optimization techniques. It responds to the limitation of existing safety approaches that require extensive manual annotation, a challenge highlighted in the safety alignment literature. The paper connects to work on jailbreak vulnerabilities and the observation that models possess latent capabilities that can be activated through appropriate prompting or training signals. It also draws on the growing understanding that reasoning models can perform self-reflection and error-correction when properly instructed, building on techniques like chain-of-thought reasoning.
enables: This approach opens pathways toward more scalable and maintainable safety systems for advanced reasoning models that require minimal human oversight. By demonstrating that models can self-generate safety-aligned responses, the work enables future research into bootstrapping safety improvements through iterative self-refinement rather than costly external annotation. The method could facilitate rapid adaptation to emerging jailbreaks and novel threat vectors since new safety cases can be identified and learned without requiring manual labeling. This work also points toward developing more efficient safety training by focusing on triggering existing model capabilities rather than teaching entirely new behaviors, potentially reducing alignment tax and opening exploration of how different trigger mechanisms affect safety performance.
